segments.text: Welcome to the session about closing the safety loop, real-time incident notifications from
all the desk format to the field.
My name is Christopher Bouter, I'm the manager of innovation at EBS and my colleague Arno
you will meet later.
EBS is a construction company based in Antwer, Belgium.
We specialize in three sectors being healthcare schools and hospitals.
We do that as a general contractor and the revenue that we turn over every year is about
70 million and we do that with about 70 to 75 people.
We work exclusively with subcontractors which makes us a different kind of general contractor
than most.
So we've all been there, you've all experienced this, you improve reporting, you standardize
templates, you clean, you finally get clean data in other desk format and then suddenly
response times slow down and issues don't get solved.
You've got problems on the job site which you log in issues for example and they don't
get there's no action being followed.
So I'm going to take you through what we encountered, why it matters, what actually happened
and what we built to solve it.
So in 2025 we started scaling safety reporting across all our construction projects.
We rolled it out in 2025 using standardized issue templates which you probably all know.
We had problems that everybody filled in some other topic in those issues and we standardized
those using these issue templates, the standard ones in ACC with a mixture of what we call
the EBS DNA.
We have a stone table that we send out to all of our subcontractors with the 10 commandments
of the safety commandments of the job site itself.
We translated those into the safety templates and we started rolling out in 2025 and we saw
an increase in data that we got into our data warehouse.
And so what we got out of it is a dashboard of which companies had which issues, which
type of issues were most commonly observed.
And so we could take actions from those templates themselves.
So for example, wearing a hard hat was the number one priority in 2025 and that's where
we made a few campaigns about.
Now some people think that having more safety issues is actually not that good.
In Belgium we need to focus on measuring those safety issues so that we can put dedicated
campaigns into the field at work.
So it's all about the iceberg concept where you measure what the type of iceberg above
the water level is to know which actually resides below the water level because not every
issue is reported.
And so we saw an increase in 2025 in all of those safety issues increasing and increasing.
We started the deployment somewhere around February March in 2025 and so you see the increase
of the incline in issues made.
And then suddenly November, October November, we saw a decline.
And so we sat down with the project leaders talking about yeah, what's going on here and
what they the feedback that they gave us was yeah, we're reporting all these issues in
other desk format.
But we're not seeing actions on the job site itself.
And so people don't look at the notifications they get from other desk format because it's
in their mailbox somewhere.
It's being sent out the next day or the next week and they don't even look at it.
So issues were being logged but no actions were being taken.
And so what they did, the project leaders was changed their method of working from creating
issues in a other desk construction cloud or other format to sending what's up messages
to those specific subcontractors where they detected the safety issues.
And so that's when we got the idea, well, why not combine it to why not have an issue
created in our desk format and have what's up message, message being sent directly to
the one assigned to the issue.
So we're about to show it who wants to see it work.
All right.
Hello everybody.
I'm Arno.
I am an in house developer at events.
And as Christopher just said, let's show you a video real quick about how this actually
works in practice.
So what we see in the video here is we are creating an issue in former.
All of the information is being filled out description, the title, your assigned user,
we are going to be adding an image in just a moment.
And when this issue gets created, what will eventually happen is we will eventually see
a WhatsApp message being sent in the WhatsApp window.
Of course, the application has to do its job.
This will take a little second before the message is going to appear.
And I will be talking a little bit more about how this all works in the background in
a moment.
But let's focus on the video right now.
So the issue has been created in a moment.
We will see this WhatsApp message getting sent in WhatsApp.
And then what's going to happen is we are also going to be replying to this message in WhatsApp.
So we are going to give it a little bit of a thumbs up and we are going to send an actual
reply following up on this issue.
And then we go back to the issue in former.
We are also going to see that this feedback that happened in WhatsApp is going to show up
in former too on the issue.
So scroll down to the comments on the issue in a sec.
We will see that the little thumbs up that we gave, the little comment that we made also
is including right now.
So now that we have shown you how this works in practice as I promised, let's also go into
how this all works, how we built this application.
Because I, as I said, I am an advanced developer at Ebenz and I was the one responsible for
developing all this.
And what I am going to do is I am not going to be diving into any code today because that's
rather boring of course.
And instead I will be talking about a few more high level things.
First of all, I will be talking about the general processes that make the application work
and to simply how does the application go from A to B.
I will also quickly be talking about the different services that we used to build the application.
And then to me at least most interestingly, I will also be diving into some intricacies
and some hurdles that we encountered during the development process.
And then what we did to tackle and solve this problem.
So let's start with the process.
As I said at the process, what I mean by that is how do we go from A to B. In this case,
is how do we go from an issue being created or updated in FormA to a WhatsApp message actually
being sent.
So what happens when an issue gets created?
Our application will get called.
But before our application actually starts, we first do some filtering.
We are going to filter for example on the type of the message or the type of the issue
rather.
We only for example want safety issues to go through.
We are also going to filter on the status of the issue and maybe some other attributes of the issue.
And what this filtering also allows us to do is make sure that for each issue only one message
gets sent.
An issue can get updated multiple times.
The application will get called multiple times and this filtering will then filter out any
consecutive times an issue gets calls the application.
So it doesn't send multiple WhatsApp messages for the same.
Now after this filtering has happened, our application can start good and well.
And what this application is then going to do is it's going to collect information.
First of all, it's going to collect information by the issue, going to collect the title of
the issue, the description, all kinds of attributes.
But also if any images are attached to this issue, it's also going to get them.
As we all know, an image speaks a thousand words.
So if there are any images attached to the issue, we also want to end up sending that along
with the message in WhatsApp.
We're not only going to get information by the issue, but we're also going to get information
by the user that is assigned to the issue.
So they can get their name and most importantly, we can get their phone number.
So we actually know where to send the message to.
Now that we have all the information, we are ready to send a message.
But what we definitely don't want to do is just dump all the information which it's got
into a message because what we really want to accomplish is we want to engage the person
that we are sending the message to to take action to provide some feedback or to take any
kind of other action.
Just dumping a lot of information on them is not going to accomplish that.
So what we actually want to do is we want to send a very human, a very personable message
and how we accomplish that is using an AI agent.
So we configured an NLM, an AI agent.
We feed all the information that we just acquired and we feed some instructions and that AI
agent will then transform all this information into, as I said, a very human, a very personable
message that will then engage that user with that person to take action to provide them
back.
And then the message that that AI informs will then get sends to WhatsApp along with any
images that we acquired from the issue itself.
Now this is actually only the first of two processes from the application because now we of
course went from A to B, we went from the issue to the WhatsApp message.
But as I just saw in the video, what also happens is that any feedback that comes from WhatsApp
will also get sent back to the issue in Forma.
And that's very important to us.
We call this closing the feedback loop.
And what we read by that is that we, any information, any feedback from WhatsApp, we want to send
that back to the issue in Forma.
To the issue in Forma actually stays somewhat like a centralized type of information that we
can use, that we can always access to get all the information.
So we went from A to B and now we also want to go from B to A.
Now I mentioned any information or any feedback in WhatsApp, we want to go back to the issue
in Forma.
What kind of feedback am I talking about?
Well first of all, there is any message status updates.
What I mean by that is, for example, is a message send, has a message been delivered,
has a message been read.
These are the little check marks that you always see at the bottom of your message in WhatsApp.
That can even from issue we want that to go back to the issue.
And then secondly, also any human feedback, any replies from the person in WhatsApp that
can be a emoji reaction, that can be a textual reply, that can be an image that they reply,
all that kind of stuff we also want to go back to the issue in Forma.
So when this thing happens, so when the status of the message updates or a reply happens
from the assigned person, our application will get called again.
And that information will get fed back into Forma in the case of the status update,
there's a specific attribute, a specific building issue that will get updated in the case
of any replies, there will be comments added on the issue.
So now that I've talked a little bit about the general processes of the application,
let's dive a little bit deeper into some problems that we encounter.
Any good developer knows during the development process, you are always going to encounter some problems,
you're going to encounter some difficulties.
And there's four things I want to talk about here that we encountered during the development process,
that we encountered, what are those things, why were there a problem, and how did we solve them?
The first thing I want to talk about has to do with something I mentioned earlier,
the only one to send one message for every issue that we create.
So we don't want to spam or contract us with multiple messages for the same,
basically what that means.
Now our original logic for this was very simple, we just used database,
and when an issue gets created or an issue gets updated,
this database is checked if the issue is already sent,
and if not the application starts, the message gets sent and the database gets up.
Now that all works great in theory,
but there is a problem that arises when an issue gets updated multiple times
in a very short span of time.
And this often happens, for example, when an issue gets created,
because an issue gets created and then after it gets created,
there's a lot of fields that get filled in, there's an image that gets attached,
so it gets updated a lot of times in a very short span of time.
And what then happens is the following, so you can kind of see it in a little graph,
I just showed you.
So the first time the application gets called everything is great,
but then the application will get called second time,
and the process will get started the second time,
but this will happen before the database actually gets updated.
So the database gets checked by the second process,
it doesn't see that message has already been sent for this issue.
So it just starts, the application just starts,
and this will eventually end up sending another message,
and this will happen third time when it gets called third time, etc.
And so what happens is a multiple messages will get sent for the same week,
and that's not what we want, as I said.
So how we solve this is we introduced a queueing system.
So instead of letting multiple processes for the same issue run in parallel,
we introduced a queue that made sure that every process only starts and only runs consecutively.
So it waits for the previous process to end,
to start the next process, and that makes sure that the previous process gets time to update the database,
and then the second process will check the database,
be that the message has already been sent and just end prematurely,
and not sent an other message.
And this queueing process is queue, then solves that issue of multiple messages,
and for the same issue.
And then we also introduced a similar queueing kind of process for the data updates and replies,
to get back into ACC to make sure that they are also handled in the correct order.
Now that we make sure that the application only starts when it needs to,
the application just starts, all works fine, or information gets gathered, and we get to our AI.
As I mentioned earlier, this AI is great, very helpful to form a very personable message,
but for anyone who has already worked with AI,
they know that it also can produce some real results.
And this case is no different.
There were, for example, the AI had a tendency to make a message longer than it needed.
We actually want very short, very direct messages,
while the AI had a little bit of a tendency to jab on, and we don't want that.
On top of that, it also has a tendency to hallucinate extra information that it was not given,
which is definitely not something we want.
It also had some problems with addressing people directly in the message.
It often had a tendency of talking to people in the third person,
while we wanted to talk to them in a first person, kind of context.
Then it also had a little bit of a tone issue.
It had a tendency of being too friendly.
It would often say things like, oh, just FYI or by the way,
which is definitely something that doesn't really fit urgency required in safety context.
And then last but not least, it also had a tendency to say,
your location or your site and the contractors,
while they were actually actually contracted to work on our site or our location.
But that was also a little bit weird.
In the main lesson, we really learned from this is garbage in garbage out.
What is more important in an AI scenario is you want to give the correct information,
but you also want to give it the correct instruction.
It does just require some time to really tune this process to really know what kind of information
do I need to give it, what kind of instructions do I need to give it,
to really get the results that you want.
Now, luckily, most of those little quirks we encountered,
we encountered them during the development process.
But even now, it has been in production for a while now.
We still encountered some weird things once in a while,
and we still spend time further tuning the AI agents to make sure that all the messages
being sent out, be it our standard.
So now that we actually have our message,
we have a message that we can send out.
We kind of encounter our following problem,
and that problem actually has to do with actually sending that message out.
Now, of course, we need a phone number to send this message out.
And the problem is that in Forma, this phone number is not always present in the user.
The user themselves are controlled their own profile,
so they are responsible for filling in their phone number,
and this doesn't always happen.
So we don't always have a phone number to send a message to.
An RKID is that we always want someone to follow up on the issue.
So we added some fallbacks to make sure that the message always ends up with someone.
First of all, when a phone number isn't found in the Forma users themselves,
we are going to check our own internal databases to see if we can find a phone number for the user
there. And if we don't find a phone number there,
then we will just send the message to our own project leader so that they can follow up on it.
Now, one last thing I want to talk about is some intricacies we have read WhatsApp themselves
or what the application itself. First of all, what is very important in WhatsApp is that we
want to prevent that any of your messages that you can send out get reported, because
any messages get reported, your account will get banned and your accounts have out any more
messages. Definitely not something won't. So we kind of introduced a no-send list.
So basically, anytime someone receives a message, they can reply, stop to that message,
and they will be put on that no-send list. And this makes sure that they won't be reporting
the other messages, because they have kind of the option to just stop the messages being sent to them.
And it also will make sure that we don't waste any resources on creating a sending messages to
people who don't want them anyway. The second thing with WhatsApp is that we need to use templates.
To send a message, we can just send any message. We need to kind of have these templates that
we send out and its templates, they get put into certain categories. There are different categories,
things like marketing, utility, these are all different categories that exist.
In WhatsApp. But they also all have a different price point. For example,
utility messages or utility template messages are cheaper than marketing.
So ideally, you want your template to be categorized in the utility category and not in the
marketing category. Now, unfortunately, we haven't been able to achieve that ourselves. The whole
process behind this is very automated and the amount of support hasn't been very helpful
until for now. So for now, our own messages are classified as marketing,
but we are still kind of looking at fines to get that utility category to make our messages cheaper.
Now, finally, I really quickly want to talk about the services that we use to build a replication.
But technically, it's very simple. Basically, we did everything in Microsoft Azure. That's a very
central application and services platform that we use here. The application itself is a function
app called the .NET that runs in Azure. The queue, storage, databases, all runs in Azure.
We also have a keyfold with any sensitive information in Azure that we create application.
The AI is an AI finally in Azure. And even WhatsApp, WhatsApp has its own API. But Azure provides
a kind of layer on top of that via its advanced messages that also allows us to use Azure to
send us WhatsApp messages. So basically, everything was built inside of Azure.
Right. So just imagine what you could do with this technology if you expand it towards
quality issues or if I notification for equipment alerts, cost anomalies, when a subcontractor
walks up to you or takes something that you can create an issue and that it automatically generates
a WhatsApp message to your project manager, for example, or your client. I think the possibilities
are endless and we are just in the brink of discovering what this integration with WhatsApp
and other disk format can do for us in the future. So thank you for listening.
And we have come to the conclusion. The end of our presentation. Thank you for listening.
